title: How to Deal with Psychic Overwhelm During Awakening
meta_description: Discover how to manage psychic overwhelm during spiritual awakening. You are not broken. Learn practical steps to protect your energy and find calm.
og_title: How to Deal with Psychic Overwhelm During Awakening
og_description: Discover how to manage psychic overwhelm during spiritual awakening. You are not broken. Learn practical steps to protect your energy and find calm.
body:
You're Not Broken. You're Just Open.

Let me guess what's happening to you right now. You're walking into a room and suddenly you feel everything - not just your own mood, but the tension between the couple at the corner table, the exhaustion of the barista, the grief your friend hasn't mentioned, and the low-grade anxiety of the person on the phone behind you. You feel it all, and it's not information. It's a weight. A pressure. A noise that won't shut off.

Maybe you've stopped going to crowded places. Maybe you've started avoiding certain people because being near them drains you for hours. Maybe you've wondered if you're losing your mind, or if something is fundamentally wrong with you.

Nothing is wrong with you. You're not broken. You're just open - and nobody told you that opening comes with a manual. This is what they don't warn you about when you start waking up. The light doesn't just show you beautiful things. It shows you everything. And everything is a lot. Right?!

I've been where you are. I've spent more nights than I can count curled up in a dark room, trying to figure out why I could feel things nobody else seemed to notice, why my nervous system would spike in a grocery store, why other people's pain would land in my chest like it was my own. So let me tell you what I learned the hard way: psychic overwhelm during awakening isn't a sign you're doing it wrong. It's a sign you're doing it right. You just need some tools to manage the volume.

"The sensitivity that feels like a curse is actually a calibration - your nervous system is learning to process a wider band of reality. The overwhelm isn't the problem. The lack of boundaries is."

Why Awakening Makes You More Sensitive (Not Less)

Here's the thing nobody tells you about spiritual awakening. When you start to wake up, your perception doesn't just add a few new channels. It opens the whole dial. You start picking up on energy, emotion, intention, and history that you were previously filtering out. And your nervous system? It wasn't built for that volume overnight.

Think of it like this. You've been living in a house with the windows painted shut. Then one day, someone comes along and pries them all open. You can finally see the sky. You can smell the trees. You can hear the birds. But you can also smell the dumpster down the alley, hear the highway three miles away, and feel the cold draft from every crack in the wall. That's awakening. The good stuff and the hard stuff come through the same opening.

Your psychic senses are expanding because your consciousness is expanding. But your nervous system hasn't caught up yet. It's still trying to process everything through the old filter system, and the old filter system is overwhelmed. That's why you feel exhausted, anxious, irritable, and sometimes just plain scared. You're not having a breakdown. You're having a bandwidth upgrade.

The key is to stop trying to shut the windows and start learning how to manage what comes through them. You don't need to go back to sleep. You need better curtains.

Grounding Isn't Optional. It's Your Reset Button.

I know you've heard this before. Ground yourself. Get in your body. Touch the earth. But I also know that when you're in the middle of psychic overwhelm, grounding can feel like the last thing you want to do. You want to float away from the noise, not sink deeper into it. I get it. But here's the truth: grounding isn't about staying in the muck. It's about giving your system a reference point so it can stop spinning.

When you're ungrounded, your energy is scattered. It's like having a radio that's picking up every station at once. Grounding is the act of tuning back to one frequency - your own. It's not about denying the other frequencies. It's about remembering which one is yours.

Start simple. Put your bare feet on the ground. Sit with your back against a tree. Hold a stone in your hand and breathe. Run cold water over your wrists. Eat something root-based - carrots, potatoes, beets. These aren't woo-woo rituals. They're physiological resets. They tell your nervous system: you are here, in this body, on this earth. The rest can wait.

I'll be honest with you. Some days, the only grounding I can manage is pressing my palms flat against my kitchen counter and taking five slow breaths. That's enough. That's always enough. You don't need an hour-long meditation. You need a moment of return. Does that land?

Your Energy Body Has a Front Door. Learn to Lock It.

One of the hardest lessons in awakening is learning that you don't have to be open to everything all the time. I used to think that being spiritually awake meant being constantly available - to people, to energy, to the collective pain of the world. That's not awakening. That's self-destruction dressed up as enlightenment.

Your energy body has boundaries just like your physical body does. You wouldn't leave your front door wide open all night and then wonder why strangers are in your living room. But that's exactly what most of us do with our energy. We leave ourselves open, and then we wonder why we feel drained, invaded, and overwhelmed.

You can learn to close down. You can learn to say no. You can learn to shield. And no, shielding isn't about building walls or becoming cold. It's about having a door that you control. You decide who and what comes in. You decide when to open and when to close. That's not spiritual bypassing. That's spiritual maturity.

Here's a practice I teach everyone who comes to me with overwhelm. Before you walk into any space - a grocery store, a meeting, a family gathering - take one breath and imagine a soft golden light around your body. Not a hard shell. A gentle boundary. Then set an intention: "I am open to what serves me. I am closed to what doesn't." That's it. You don't have to feel the boundary. You just have to set it. Your energy body knows what to do.

For a deeper dive into this, I highly recommend Sonya Renee Taylor's "The Body Is Not an Apology". It's not a psychic book, but it's one of the best resources I've found for understanding how to inhabit your own body without apology - which is exactly what you need when your sensitivity makes you want to disappear.

The Difference Between Your Pain and Their Pain

Here's the most common question I get from people in the thick of psychic overwhelm: "How do I know if this feeling is mine or someone else's?" And the answer is both simple and maddening. Simple because there's a clear test. Maddening because it takes practice.

The test is this: your pain has a signature. It has a texture, a location, a quality that you can learn to recognize. Other people's pain feels different. It might be sharper, duller, higher or lower in your body. It might come with images or words that aren't yours. It might feel like it belongs to someone else - because it does.

Start by checking in with yourself multiple times a day when you're alone and calm. Notice what your baseline feels like. Where do you hold tension? What's your default emotional temperature? What does your energy feel like when nothing is happening? Once you know your own signature, you'll start to notice when something doesn't match. That's the first step in sorting what's yours from what isn't.

When you feel a spike of emotion or physical sensation that doesn't fit, ask yourself: "Did this feeling start before or after I walked into this room? Before or after this person started talking? Before or after I opened my phone?" If it started after, there's a good chance it's not yours. And you can let it go. You don't have to process it. You don't have to fix it. You just have to recognize it and release it.

This is one of the most freeing skills you'll ever learn. You are not responsible for carrying everything you feel. Some of it is just passing through. Let it.

Your Nervous System Needs a Different Kind of Care

Most self-care advice is designed for people who are under-stimulated. You're not under-stimulated. You're over-stimulated. So bubble baths and candles aren't going to cut it. You need care that actually addresses your nervous system's state.

What works for overwhelm is different from what works for burnout or depression. Overwhelm means your system is flooded. You need to drain the flood, not add more water. So instead of adding more sensory input, you need to reduce it. Instead of trying to feel better, you need to feel less. At least for a while.

Here's what I recommend. Blackout curtains. Noise-canceling headphones. A weighted blanket. Minimal social media. No news before bed. One meal a day eaten in silence. A walk without music or podcasts. These aren't punishments. They're medicine for a system that's been on high alert for too long.

You might also need to change how you eat. When your nervous system is overwhelmed, it craves sugar and carbs because they provide quick energy. But that quick energy spikes your system even more. Try adding more protein and fat to your meals. Drink more water. Cut back on caffeine. I know that last one hurts. But caffeine is an amplifier, and right now you don't need amplification. You need dampening.

If you're looking for a practical guide to nervous system regulation, Deb Dana's "Polyvagal Theory in Therapy" is worth your time. It's written for clinicians, but the exercises are accessible and they work. You don't need to understand the theory to benefit from the practices.

You Don't Have to Fix Everything You Feel

This might be the hardest lesson of all. When you're sensitive, you feel things deeply. And when you feel things deeply, you want to do something about them. You want to fix the pain, solve the problem, heal the wound. But here's the truth: not everything you feel needs your action. Some feelings just need your presence.

You can feel grief without trying to make it go away. You can feel anger without acting on it. You can feel someone else's pain without taking responsibility for it. Feeling is not doing. And you don't have to do anything about most of what you feel. Your job is to notice, to allow, and then to let go. That's it.

I know this sounds simple, but it's not easy. Especially when you're used to being the one who holds everything, who absorbs everything, who tries to fix everything. But fixing isn't your job. Your job is to be present. And presence doesn't require action. It requires stillness.

Try this the next time you're overwhelmed. Sit down. Put your hand on your heart. Say to yourself: "I don't have to fix this. I just have to feel this." Then breathe. Let the feeling be there without trying to change it. You might be surprised how much of the overwhelm dissolves when you stop fighting it.

Building a Daily Practice That Actually Works

You don't need a complicated spiritual practice. You need a consistent one. Five minutes a day is better than two hours once a month. Here's what I recommend for people in the middle of psychic overwhelm.

Morning: Before you check your phone, put your feet on the floor and take three breaths. Set an intention for how you want to feel today. Not what you want to accomplish. How you want to feel. That's your north star.

Midday: Check in with yourself. Rate your overwhelm from 1 to 10. If it's above a 6, do something to bring it down. Step outside. Splash water on your face. Shake your hands out. You don't have to wait until you're drowning to reach for a life raft.

Evening: Create a shutdown ritual. Turn off screens an hour before bed. Write down anything you're holding onto. Then imagine placing it in a box and closing the lid. You can pick it up tomorrow. Tonight, you rest.

The most important part of any practice is that it's yours. Don't copy what someone else does. Experiment. Find what works for your body, your schedule, your nervous system. And if you miss a day, don't beat yourself up. Just come back tomorrow. This is a practice, not a performance.

For a deeper look at how to build a sustainable spiritual practice that honors your sensitivity, Kyle Gray's "Raise Your Vibration" is a solid resource. It's practical, direct, and doesn't ask you to be anything other than what you are.

When to Seek Help (And Why It's Not a Failure)

Let me be clear about something. Psychic overwhelm can sometimes be a sign of something deeper - trauma, anxiety, depression, or a nervous system that needs professional support. And if that's the case, spiritual practices alone won't fix it. You might need therapy. You might need medication. You might need both. And that's not a failure. That's wisdom.

I've worked with too many people who tried to spiritualize their way out of a clinical condition. They meditated harder. They chanted more. They convinced themselves that if they just had more faith, the darkness would lift. And it didn't. Because the darkness wasn't spiritual. It was chemical. It was neurological. It was trauma stored in the body that needed a trained professional to help release it.

If your overwhelm is affecting your ability to eat, sleep, work, or connect with people you love, please seek help. There is nothing unspiritual about getting support. In fact, it might be the most spiritual thing you can do - honoring your body enough to give it what it actually needs.

And if you're working with a therapist, look for someone who understands sensitivity. Not all therapists do. You might need someone trained in somatic experiencing, EMDR, or sensorimotor psychotherapy. These modalities work with the body, not just the mind. And for someone with psychic overwhelm, body-based work is often more effective than talk therapy alone.

One book that helped me understand the intersection of sensitivity and trauma is Bessel van der Kolk's "The Body Keeps the Score". It's a heavy read, but it will change how you understand your own nervous system. And understanding is the first step to healing.

The Other Side of Overwhelm

I want to tell you something that might be hard to believe right now. This overwhelm you're feeling? It's not the end of the story. It's the middle. And the middle is always the hardest part. But there's an other side.

On the other side of overwhelm, there's clarity. There's ease. There's the ability to feel deeply without being destroyed by what you feel. There's the gift of your sensitivity - the way you can read a room, comfort a friend, sense danger before it arrives, and connect with something larger than yourself. That's all still there. It's just buried under the noise right now.

You don't have to push through. You don't have to white-knuckle your way to enlightenment. You just have to take the next right step. Ground yourself. Set a boundary. Let go of what isn't yours. Get some sleep. Eat something real. Ask for help if you need it. That's the path. It's not glamorous. But it works.

And one day - sooner than you think - you'll look back at this time and realize it was the crucible that forged you. Not despite the overwhelm, but because of it. Because you learned to hold yourself in the fire. And that changes everything.

If you're ready to go deeper into understanding your sensitivity and how to work with it instead of against it, I've put together a free assessment that can help you identify where you are in your awakening and what you need most right now. Take the assessment here - it takes about ten minutes, and it might give you the clarity you've been looking for.
